codeReview.summary: Solid implementation that covers all six expectedBehavior items. The drawing library additions (render_cursor, scale_pointer_coord, CURSOR_BITMAP) are clean, well-documented, and thoroughly tested. The compositor integration correctly adds a z=30 cursor surface, tracks pointer position from MSG_POINTER_ABS, generates targeted dirty rects for old+new cursor positions rather than full-screen, and introduces a text_changed flag to avoid unnecessary content re-renders on mouse-only movement. The coordinate scaling function uses u64 intermediate math to prevent overflow, divides by 32768 (not 32767) to keep results strictly below max_pixels, and has a defensive clamp. The cursor bitmap is a recognizable classic arrow shape. 9 new tests cover rendering correctness and coordinate scaling edge cases including zero-max-pixels. The composite_surfaces_rect function already handles boundary clipping so cursor at screen edges won't cause out-of-bounds writes. No blocking issues found.

issues.description: Dirty rects for cursor are added with u16 coordinates (damage.add casts MOUSE_X/Y to u16). While fb_width/height are typically <=1920/1080 (well within u16 range), the code performs raw 'as u16' truncation without explicit bounds checking. If the framebuffer were ever >65535 pixels in either dimension, dirty rects would wrap silently. Not a practical concern for current hardware but worth noting as a latent assumption.

When the cursor is at the bottom-right edge of the screen (e.g., x=1268, y=1064 on a 1280x1080 display), the dirty rect (x, y, 12, 16) extends past the framebuffer boundary. The DamageTracker.add() stores the rect as-is without clamping, but composite_surfaces_rect does clamp to dst bounds. So no out-of-bounds write occurs, but the GPU transfer dirty rect could reference pixels beyond the framebuffer. Whether this matters depends on the GPU driver's handling of out-of-bounds transfer rects. Since the compositor already uses composite_surfaces_rect (which clamps), the compositing itself is safe.

The MSG_POINTER_ABS handler sets PREV_MOUSE_X/Y before updating MOUSE_X/Y, which is correct. However, if multiple MSG_POINTER_ABS messages arrive in a single event loop iteration (rapid mouse movement), only the last position is rendered. The dirty rect for intermediate positions is lost — only the first old position and last new position get dirty rects. This is actually fine for cursor rendering (you only want the final position) but means intermediate dirty rects from overwritten PREV_MOUSE positions don't get generated. In practice: if the cursor moves A→B→C in one frame, only A and C get dirty rects, not B. Pixels at B that had the cursor composited won't get cleaned until the next frame that dirties that region. This is a very minor visual artifact (a ghost cursor for one frame in a fast-swipe scenario).

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker used a temporary CURSOR_VISIBLE=true hack to force cursor rendering for visual verification, since QEMU doesn't route mouse events to virtio-tablet in headless mode. The os-worker SKILL.md documents 'mouse_move' via HMP for mouse testing but doesn't mention the temporary-visible-cursor workaround. Worker's skillFeedback explicitly suggests adding this guidance.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff interactiveChecks[0]: 'temporarily set CURSOR_VISIBLE=true and MOUSE_X/Y=400/300 to force cursor rendering'. skillFeedback.suggestedChanges[0]: 'Add guidance to os-worker skill about the temporary-CURSOR_VISIBLE hack'.

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md already documents that QEMU doesn't route mouse events in headless mode and that pointer assertions should use code review + unit tests. The worker followed this guidance correctly. AGENTS.md also already mentions the qemu-mouse service config (services.yaml has it). No convention gap found here.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: AGENTS.md 'Visual Verification' section explicitly covers this limitation for milestone 3.

sharedStateObservations.observation: No gap. The worker used the standard qemu-mouse service configuration from services.yaml which already includes -device virtio-tablet-device.

summary: Pass. Clean, well-structured implementation fully covering all six expected behaviors: z=30 cursor surface, pointer tracking from MSG_POINTER_ABS, visibility over all surfaces, efficient dirty rects (not full screen), recognizable arrow shape, and all existing tests passing (896 total, 9 new). Three non-blocking observations: u16 truncation on damage rect coordinates (safe for current resolutions), dirty rects not clamped to framebuffer bounds before storage (safe due to downstream clamping in compositor), and potential single-frame ghost cursor artifact during rapid multi-event movement. None require action. Worker followed skill procedure with a justified deviation for visual verification.
